blurb: The long-blooming false sunflower: cheerful golden daisies (with golden, not dark, centers) from early summer clear to frost on a sturdy clump. Easier and longer-blooming than a true sunflower, and a magnet for bees and butterflies.
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
seasons: Golden daisies with golden centers from early summer to frost — very long blooming; toothed foliage
wildlife: A specialist-bee host plus nectar for many bees and butterflies; seeds feed birds; a caterpillar host

care.hardiness: A vigorous, long-lived native of central and eastern North America, hardy roughly USDA zones 3–9, so it is fully cold-hardy in Boulder (zone 5b–6a) and dies back to the crown each winter.[1][2] More adaptable to our climate than a true sunflower, it returns reliably from a sturdy clump.[3]
care.planting: Set out container-grown plants in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall while soil is workable; water in to establish. It is widely adaptable but flowers best in full sun and well-drained ground. In pots use a fast-draining mix and water more often — containers dry fast in our heat and wind — and protect the crown from prolonged winter wet.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun is best; it tolerates part shade, but plants in shade grow weaker, floppier stems and bloom less.[1][2]
care.soil: Adaptable — grows in clay, loam, or sandy soils across a wide pH range (roughly 6.0–8.0), so Boulder's alkaline clay is no problem; it tolerates poor, dry ground but performs best in average, well-drained soil.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate; highly drought-tolerant once established, though less strictly xeric than the prairie golden asters — give occasional deep soakings in a hot, dry spell to keep the long bloom going.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants about 18–24 in. apart; the sturdy 3–4 ft clumps rarely need staking in full sun, though tall stems in shade or rich soil may flop.[1][2]
care.propagation: Easiest by division of the clump in spring or fall, and readily grown from seed; it also takes stem cuttings. Seed germinates best after a cold-moist stratification, supplied by a fall outdoor sowing or several weeks chilled in the refrigerator before spring sowing.[1][2]
care.sow: Sow in fall outdoors to let winter break dormancy, or stratify and sow in early spring; it self-sows readily and seedlings can flower their first or second year.[1][2]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify the seed (a fall outdoor sowing, or a few weeks at ~40°F before a spring sowing) to improve germination.[1]
care.bloom: Very long blooming — golden daisies with golden centers from early summer into fall, roughly June through September on the Front Range; deadheading prolongs it.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Deadhead spent flowers to extend the bloom and limit self-sowing; an optional cutback by one-third in late spring keeps plants shorter and bushier. Cut stems back in late winter, but leave some seedheads standing for the birds.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Self-sows readily where happy; deadheading curbs it, and unwanted seedlings are easy to pull or move.[1][2]
care.troubles: No serious problems; the minor ones are aphids (which can cluster on the soft new growth) and powdery mildew in crowded, poorly aired plantings — good spacing and full sun keep both in check.[1][2]
care.companions: Combines well with other sun-loving prairie perennials — switchgrass and little bluestem, asters, coneflowers, and bee balm — in a meadow or pollinator border.[2]

edible.summary: Grown as a native ornamental and pollinator/wildlife plant, not for eating; no established culinary use and not known to be poisonous to people.[1][2]
edible.caution: False sunflower has no documented food use, so it is treated as inedible — don't eat any part. It is not known to be notably poisonous, but absence of food documentation is not a green light, and as with any daisy-family (Asteraceae) plant you should never eat one without certain identification.[1][2]
